analogy: Think of "Introduction" through the chapter's own opening images: Chapter 1 Introduction Introduction Introduction Introduction Introduction 1.1 A SIMPLE ECONOMY Think of any society. People in the society need many goods and services1 in their everyday life including food, clothing, shelter, transport facilities like roads and railways, postal services and various other services like... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 1.1 A SIMPLE ECONOMY, 1.2 CENTRAL PROBLEMS OF AN ECONOMY, 3 Introduction, EXAMPLE 1. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Introduction is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that chapter 1 Introduction Introduction Introduction Introduction Introduction 1.1 A SIMPLE ECONOMY Think of any society.

The text explains that people in the society need many goods and services1 in their everyday life including food, clothing, shelter, transport facilities like roads and railways, postal services and various other services like that of teachers and doctors.

The text explains that in fact, the list of goods and services that any individual2 needs is so large that no individual in society, to begin with, has all the things she needs.

The text explains that every individual has some amount of only a few of the goods and services that she would like to use.

The text explains that a family farm may own a plot of land, some grains, farming implements, maybe a pair of bullocks and also the labour services of the family members.

The text explains that a weaver may have some yarn, some cotton and other instruments required for weaving cloth.

The text explains that the teacher in the local school has the skills required to impart education to the students.

The text explains that some others in society may not have any resource3 excepting their own labour services.

The text explains that each of these decision making units can produce some goods or services by using the resources that it has and use part of the produce to obtain the many other goods and services which it needs.

The text explains that for example, the family farm can produce corn, use part of the produce for consumption purposes and procure clothing, housing and various services in exchange for the rest of the produce.

The text explains that similarly, the weaver can get the goods and services that she wants in exchange for the cloth she produces in her yarn.

The text explains that the teacher can earn some money by teaching students in the school and use the money for obtaining the goods and services that she wants.

The text explains that the labourer also can try to fulfill her needs by using whatever money she can earn by working for someone else.

The text explains that each individual can thus use her resources to fulfill her needs.

The text explains that it goes without saying that no individual has unlimited resources compared to her needs.

The text explains that the amount of corn that the family farm can produce is limited by the amount of resources it has, and hence, the amount of different goods 1By goods we means physical, tangible objects used to satisfy people’s wants and needs.

The text explains that 3By resource, we mean those goods and services which are used to produce other goods and services, e.g. land, labour, tools and machinery, etc.

The text explains that the same is the case with all other individuals in society.

The text explains that everyone faces scarcity of resources, and therefore, has to use the limited resources in the best possible way to fulfill her needs.

The text explains that in general, every individual in society is engaged in the production of some goods or services and she wants a combination of many goods and services not all of which are produced by her.

The text explains that needless to say that there has to be some compatibility between what people in society collectively want to have and what they produce4.

The text explains that for example, the total amount of corn produced by family farm along with other farming units in a society must match the total amount of corn that people in the society collectively want to consume.

The text explains that if people in the society do not want as much corn as the farming units are capable of producing collectively, a part of the resources of these units could have been used in the production of some other good or services which is in high demand.

The text explains that on the other hand, if people in the society want more corn compared to what the farming units are producing collectively, the resources used in the production of some other goods and services may be reallocated to the production of corn.

The text explains that just as the resources of an individual are scarce, the resources of the society are also scarce in comparison to what the people in the society might collectively want to have.

The text explains that the scarce resources of the society have to be allocated properly in the production of different goods and services in keeping with the likes and dislikes of the people of the society.

The text explains that any allocation5 of resources of the society would result in the production of a particular combination of different goods and services.

The text explains that the goods and services thus produced will have to be distributed among the individuals of the society.

The text explains that the allocation of the limited resources and the distribution of the final mix of goods and services are two of the basic economic problems faced by the society.

The text explains that in reality, any economy is much more complex compared to the society discussed above.

The text explains that in the light of what we have learnt about the society, let us now discuss the fundamental concerns of the discipline of economics some of which we shall study throughout this book.

The text explains that 1.2 CENTRAL PROBLEMS OF AN ECONOMY Production, exchange and consumption of goods and services are among the basic economic activities of life.

The text explains that in the course of these basic economic activities, every society has to face scarcity of resources and it is the scarcity of resources that gives rise to the problem of choice.

The text explains that the scarce resources of an economy have competing usages.

The text explains that in other words, every society has to decide on how to use its scarce resources.

The text explains that the problems of an economy are very often summarised as follows: 4Here we assume that all the goods and services produced in a society are consumed by the people in the society and that there is no scope of getting anything from outside the society.

The text explains that 5By an allocation of the resources, we mean how much of which resource is devoted to the production of each of the goods and services.

The text explains that every society must decide on how much of each of the many possible goods and services it will produce.

The text explains that whether to use more resources in education and health or to use more resources in building military services.

The text explains that every society has to decide on how much of which of the resources to use in the production of each of the different goods and services.

The text explains that who gets how much of the goods that are produced in the economy?

The text explains that how should the produce of the economy be distributed among the individuals in the economy?

The text explains that whether or not to ensure a minimum amount of consumption for everyone in the economy.

The text explains that whether or not elementary education and basic health services should be available freely for everyone in the economy.

The text explains that 7 Introduction K K K K Key Conceptsey Concepts ey Concepts ey Concepts ey Concepts Consumption Production Exchange Scarcity Production possibilities Opportunity cost Market Market economy Centrally planned economy Mixed economy Positive analysis Normative analysis Microeconomics Macroeconomics ? ?

The text explains that what do you mean by the production possibilities of an economy?

The text explains that distinguish between a centrally planned economy and a market economy.

The text explains that what do you understand by positive economic analysis?

The text explains that what do you understand by normative economic analysis?

The text explains that distinguish between microeconomics and macroeconomics.

The text explains that chapter 3 deals with basic ideas of production and cost.

The text explains that in Chapter 4, we study the producer’s behaviour.

Source: NCERT Class 12 Introductory Microeconomics, Chapter 1: Introduction

Chapter 1 Introduction Introduction Introduction Introduction Introduction 1.1 A SIMPLE ECONOMY Think of any society.
For example, the family farm can produce corn, use part of the produce for consumption purposes and procure clothing, housing and various services in exchange for the rest...
A decision making unit can be a single person or a group like a household, a firm or any other organisation.
1.3.1 The Centrally Planned Economy In a centrally planned economy, the government or the central authority plans all the important activities in the economy.
Chapter 1 Introduction Introduction Introduction Introduction Introduction 1.1 A SIMPLE ECONOMY Think of any society.
1.3.2 The Market Economy In contrast to a centrally planned economy, in a market economy, all economic activities are organised through the market.
1.4 POSITIVE AND NORMATIVE ECONOMICS It was mentioned earlier that in principle there are more than one ways of solving the central problems of an economy.

prelims_traps: Do not treat 1.1 A SIMPLE ECONOMY and 1.2 CENTRAL PROBLEMS OF AN ECONOMY as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Introduction; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Introduction through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 1.1 A SIMPLE ECONOMY and 1.2 CENTRAL PROBLEMS OF AN ECONOMY to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
